title: India's Long-Haul Push Reshapes Gulf and Indian-Ocean Routes
dek: As Indian carriers add wide-body capacity and deepen Gulf ties, the subcontinent is emerging as one of the most contested long-haul markets in Asia, with Air India, IndiGo and Gulf hubs all competing for connecting traffic.
body:
India entered the second half of 2025 as one of the fastest-growing aviation markets in the world, and its network map is being redrawn accordingly. With a rapidly expanding middle class, a swelling diaspora and a government keen to position Delhi, Mumbai and Bengaluru as international gateways, Indian carriers and their Gulf rivals are competing intensively for both point-to-point and connecting long-haul demand.

Wide-bodies return to the subcontinent

Air India, under Tata Group ownership, has been working through a multi-year fleet renewal anchored by large Airbus and Boeing orders placed in 2023, while gradually refurbishing inherited wide-bodies. The strategy aims to recapture nonstop traffic to North America and Europe that has long leaked to one-stop itineraries through the Gulf and Europe.

IndiGo, historically a narrow-body low-cost specialist, has signalled longer-range ambitions through wide-body commitments and damp-leased aircraft, testing demand on medium and long-haul sectors that complement its dominant domestic feed.

The Gulf hubs fight to hold connecting share

Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad have built much of their success on funnelling Indian passengers over Dubai, Doha and Abu Dhabi. Expanded bilateral entitlements and additional frequencies to Indian metros remain a recurring point of negotiation, because every nonstop an Indian carrier adds is potential connecting traffic the Gulf super-connectors could lose.

  • Nonstop growth from DEL, BOM and BLR to long-haul markets
  • Continued reliance on DXB and DOH for one-stop connectivity
  • Bilateral capacity as a lever in India-Gulf air negotiations

The outcome is unlikely to be winner-take-all. India's outbound market is large enough to support both more nonstops and robust connecting flows, but the balance of where passengers change planes is genuinely in play. For now, capacity discipline, aircraft availability and the pace of Air India's integration with the former Vistara operation will shape how quickly the subcontinent's long-haul ambitions translate into sustained schedules.
heroImageAlt: A wide-body airliner climbing after takeoff against a clear sky, illustrating India's expanding long-haul network
metaTitle: India Long-Haul Push Reshapes Gulf, Indian-Ocean Routes
metaDescription: Indian carriers add wide-body capacity and deepen Gulf ties in 2025, turning the subcontinent into one of Asia's most contested long-haul and connecting markets.
